retrieved_docs.title: Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography -- Indications -- Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography Versus Fluorescein Angiography and Indocyanine Green Angiography
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary retinal dystrophies: Inherited retinal conditions such as retinitis pigmentosa can be monitored using OCTA to assess vascular changes, particularly capillary dropout and retinal atrophy. [33] Ocular ischemic syndrome: OCTA is indicated for identifying ischemic changes in the retinal and choroidal vasculature in patients with carotid artery disease or other vascular disorders affecting ocular blood flow. [34] Coats disease : In pediatric and rare vascular conditions like Coats disease, OCTA is used to visualize abnormal retinal vasculature and detect associated exudates. [35] Idiopathic polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy : OCTA helps diagnose and monitor polypoidal lesions in the choroid, which can lead to subretinal hemorrhage and fluid accumulation. [36] Retinal and choroidal tumors : OCTA can assist in assessing the vascular network in ocular tumors, helping to differentiate between benign and malignant lesions. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: PCV may induce choroidal ischemia, inflammation, RPE damage, and breaks in Bruch's membrane. These changes can contribute to the formation of CNVM, fibrosis, and subsequent scarring. The ultimate consequence of this sequela is irreversible vision loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Wet Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral exudative hemorrhagic chorioretinopathy is a disease affecting older individuals characterized by peripheral subretinal or sub-RPE hemorrhage or exudates that may sometimes reach the posterior pole. [105] Many such patients are hypertensive and on blood-thinning agents. This entity may be a variant of PCV, and around 60% of patients may show polyps on ICGA. [106] Treatment options include anti-VEGF agents, laser, PDT, pars plana vitrectomy, and cryotherapy. Peripheral exudative hemorrhagic chorioretinopathy may simulate choroidal melanoma. [107]

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pathophysiology of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy. Assess the risk factors for developing vision loss in this condition. Evaluate the indications for the treatment of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy. Summarize interprofessional strategies that enhance collaboration, optimize treatment planning, and improve patient outcomes in the management of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: FFA must be performed at the initial presentation to identify leakage from the abnormal vascular network. Leakage on FFA is a sign of disease activity. Fluorescein angiography has a limited role in diagnosing polyps. Moreover, it overestimates the lesion size, compared to ICGA, if a PED is present along the edge of the lesion because, in FFA, PED appears hyperfluorescent.

retrieved_docs.title: Indocyanine Green (ICG) Angiography -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The indications of ICGA include: Exudative age-related macular degeneration (wet AMD) [21] Idiopathic polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (IPCV or PCV) [22] Retinal angiomatous proliferation (RAP) [23] Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR) [10] Retinal arterial macroaneurysm (RAM) [24] Choroidal tumors Choroidal melanoma Choroidal hemangioma White Dot Syndromes [25] Birdshot chorioretinopathy (BSCR) Multifocal choroiditis Multiple evanescent white dot syndrome (MEWDS) Serpiginous choroidopathy Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE) Punctate inner chorioretinopathy (PIC) Acute zonal occult outer retinopathy (AZOOR) Chorioretinal atrophy Ocular inflammatory diseases Sympathetic ophthalmia (SO) [26] Vogt Koyanagi Harada syndrome (VKH) [27] ICG is also used to determine liver function, hepatic blood flow, and cardiac output. ICGA has been used intraoperatively during the surgical management of intracranial aneurysms. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV) is an exudative maculopathy characterized by multiple recurrent serosanguineous retinal pigment epithelial detachments (PED). The cause of PCV is considered to be due to inner choroidal vessel abnormalities. These vessels have multiple, small, polyp-shaped aneurysms that form due to a defective vascular lining. These vessels have thinned out endothelial cells and reduced pericytes compared to normal vasculature. It is similar to neovascular age-related macular degeneration in morphological characteristics. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy and highlights the interprofessional team's role in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Neovascular AMD: PCV shares many similarities with nAMD. It has similar clinical features, increased VEGF expression, similar histology, and expression of growth factors and receptor antibodies. However, PCV has now been accepted as a distinct entity. The VEGF levels in PCV are less than nAMD or myopic CNVM. The clinical signs of PCV are also different from nAMD. The polyps project under the surface of RPE from the plane of the inner choroid. RPE invariably remains intact, unlike nAMD, where neovascular tufts arising from choroid breach Bruch's membrane and grow in sub-RPE or subretinal space. OCT findings of sharp-peaked/ notched PED or double-layer signs distinguish PCV from nAMD.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy is similar in presentation to neovascular AMD. Due to the significant morbidity associated with PCV, timely diagnosis and management are necessary to preserve vision. Sudden onset of diminution or distortion of vision must be evaluated with OCT, and anti-VEGF therapy must be started to prevent the onset of fibrosis and permanent scarring. Patients must be educated about treatment compliance. Treatment such as PDT or thermal laser may reduce or eliminate the need for monthly intravitreal injections, thereby reducing the cost of treatment to the patients. Educators should stress to patients that any worsening of vision in either eye needs to be assessed promptly because delays in treatment can lead to irreversible vision loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: ICGA is a preferred imaging modality compared to FFA for PCV because indocyanine green dye absorbs and emits near-infrared light that penetrates RPE, leading to better imaging of choroidal structures. Moreover, unlike fluorescein dye, ICG doesn't leak from choriocapillaris, so choroidal lesions are less obscured. ICGA is the gold standard for diagnosing PCV and differentiating it from retinal angiomatous proliferation and typical AMD. The ICGA characteristics of PCV are abnormal vascular network (AVN) of inner choroidal vessels, nodular dilatations at the edge of AVN, and hyperfluorescent halo around polyps, arising within the first 6 minutes. Pulsation of polyps can be observed using video ICGA.

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: OCT shows certain distinctive features that help a clinician suspect PCV. It is not diagnostic alone but should be used as an adjuvant to ICGA, disease monitoring, and assessment of response to treatment. Distinctive OCT features suggestive of PCV include sharp-peaked PED, notched PED, hyporeflective lumen surrounded by hyperreflective ring under the surface of RPE, and double-layer sign. A double-layer sign implies that RPE is distinct from Bruch's membrane in spectral-domain OCT and represents the presence of AVN. Sharp peaked PED denotes polyp. The presence of thickened choroid (pachychoroid) on enhanced depth imaging-OCT supports the diagnosis of PCV and rules out typical AMD where the underlying choroid is usually thin.

retrieved_docs.title: Macular Degeneration -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy can be differentiated on fundus examination by the presence of orangish nodules along with subretinal hemorrhage or fluid. OCT shows large choroidal vessels, also known as pachyvessels, in these cases. The choroid is thickened, unlike ARMD, where the choroid is thin. ICGA shows polyps that are diagnostic of PCV.

retrieved_docs.title: Gyrate Atrophy of the Choroid and Retina -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding the ophthalmic evaluation of the condition , fluorescein angiography of patients with GA shows prominent retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) transmission window defects which start in the peripheral retina and correspond to the areas of RPE and choriocapillaris atrophy that characterize the disease with a hyperfluorescent lining along the borders of these defects. Large choroidal vessels are clearly seen through these defects. These defects gradually enlarge in size on follow-up and progress to coalesce together. [5] Some patients also have cystoid macular edema associated with macular leakage. Others, such as patients with foveoschisis, do not have macular leakage. [22] Later, the macula can also be involved with chorioretinal atrophy. [1] [49] Fluorescein angiography can also demonstrate leakage in cases of GA complicated by choroidal neovascularization. [50] [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with PCV  present with a diminution of vision. Other symptoms at presentation include metamorphopsia, central scotoma, and floaters. Patients presenting within 3 months of onset have better visual acuity and clinically have signs of subretinal exudation or hemorrhage. The presence of lipid exudation or intraretinal cysts is a sign of chronicity. [9] PCV cases tend to have better visual acuity at presentation than typical AMD cases. [10] Better visual acuity in PCV could be due to extrafoveal location and minimal intraretinal changes. Long-standing cases show signs of subretinal fibrosis, pigment epithelial hyperplasia, or atrophic retinal changes.

Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

Post‑inflammatory and postoperative sequelae comprise post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and Dutch‑language entries litteken; choroidea and litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief. Specific entities include acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد, APMPPE) and various choroidal lesions (litteken; choroidea). All these conditions share common anatomic involvement of the choroid and retina and often overlap in pathophysiology such as inflammation, ischemia, systemic vascular disease, trauma, or postoperative healing, necessitating integrated clinical assessment, imaging, and management. Additionally, the condition حثل المشيمية

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: epitheliopathy (اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد (APMPPE)) is an inflammatory placoid disorder of the choroid and retinal pigment epithelium. Familial choroidal shape anomalies (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية) reflect a hereditary structural abnormality of the choroid. Various forms of choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية) and unspecified focal choroiditis with retinal involvement (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد) further illustrate inflammatory disease at this site. Degenerative and exudative processes include serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Calcific changes are noted as choroidal calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) is the anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular diseases and lesions. Inflammatory conditions that involve the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, as well as nonspecific choroiditis, choroiditis‑retinitis, and focal choroiditis‑retinitis of unspecified etiology. Degenerative changes affecting the choroid are represented by age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal degeneration, and the acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE). Traumatic and postoperative processes also manifest in the choroid, producing post‑inflammatory, post‑surgical, and post‑traumatic choroidal scars. Additional choroidal findings include serous choroidal detachment (including serous detachment of the eye), choroidal inversion, generalized choroidal calcification, and hereditary choroidal calcification. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s role as a common locus for inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, traumatic, and iatrogenic ocular pathology, highlighting the importance of recognizing choroidal involvement in the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of diverse eye diseases.
